narrative: Dear [Student's Name], as a Black woman applying to law school, you've shown determination in pursuing your dream despite the challenges many face in this field, and I want to help you understand the automated decision that led to your application's rejection in a clear way. The admissions model looks at past students' profiles—like grades, test scores, family background, and study plans—to predict if someone is likely to pass the bar exam on their first try, and unfortunately, it estimated a 90% chance that you would not, which triggered the denial to protect the school's outcomes. Your Year 3 school decile ranking of 1st—far below the typical 5th or 6th decile—had the strongest pull toward this failure prediction, signaling to the model that your later academic performance might not build the foundation needed for the bar. Right behind that, your LSAT score of 19.3, well under the average of around 37, significantly reinforced the model's concern about your readiness for the rigors ahead. Your Year 1 decile of 1st also contributed notably in the same direction, suggesting early struggles that carried weight in the overall assessment. Other factors like your middle family income bracket played a smaller role in pushing toward failure, while your undergraduate GPA of 2.3 had minimal influence, and choosing full-time studies was a slight positive that nudged things toward success—but not enough to offset the top concerns. Compared to typical students who get approved, your deciles and LSAT stand out as much lower than averages, putting you in a tougher spot statistically. This isn't a judgment on your potential or the barriers you've overcome as a Black woman in education; it's just how this predictive tool weighed your specifics against historical patterns. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost that score, strengthening your later-year grades through targeted prep or additional courses, and perhaps exploring programs with bar passage support tailored for diverse students like you. Many have turned similar predictions around with focused effort—reach out to advisors for personalized strategies, and keep pushing; your story is far from over. You've got this resilience; use it to rewrite the next chapter.
